paragraphs.text: THE INFLUENCE OF THE SPIRIT having those forces and actions indicated previously. But that is not the subject of our discussion now. The human soul, being such as has been described, displays aspirations above all this and rises a step further, because it is an inspired soul.
Such inspirations are apparent in every aspect of the soul’s life, the intellectual, active and sensual.

In the mental part, the yearning for the ideal in the soul appears from the action of the spirit. In fact, the entire process of the intellect is guided by experience and observation. From what it learns haphazardly and disconnectedly by this method, the soul forms generalizations, draws conclusions, and thus obtains basic suppositions about a known sphere of things. This would be what it stands on. At the same time, it is never satisfied with this, but strives higher, seeking to define the significance of each sphere ofthings in the overall sum of creation. For example, we know what man is from observations of him, generalizations about him and conclusions we have made. Not being content with this, however, we come up with the question, “What is the significance of man in the sum of creation.^ ” In trying to discover this, one person decides that man is the head and crown of creation. Another decides that he is the high priest, in the sense that the voices of all creation praise God instinctively, while man raises praise to the Creator Above All with rational song. The soul is inclined to generate concepts of this type, both concerning every other type of creature, and concerning creation as a whole. So does it generate. Whether these concepts correspond to reality or not is another question, but without a doubt the soul is inclined to seek these concepts; it seeks and generates them. This, then, is the yearning for the ideal, for the meaning of a thing is its idea.
This yearning is universal to everyone. Even those who do not place any value on any kind of knowledge other than that from experience, without noticing it are unable to refrain from 67

THE SPIRITUAL LIFE idealizing against their will. They reject ideas with their tongues, but build them in actual fact. They accept conjectures, which are the lowest class of knowledge, but without which we could not have a single domain of knowledge.

Metaphysics and true philosophy, which have always been and always will be in the realm of human knowledge, are the ideal manner of viewing things. The spirit which is ever present within us as a significant force, itself contemplating God as Creator and Providence, also draws the soul into that invisible and boundless realm. Perhaps the spirit, by its Divine likeness, was intended to contemplate all things in God: it would do so, if there had been no Fall. But nowadays it is necessary for anyone who wishes to contemplate all reality ideally to proceed from God or from that Credo, which was written by God in the spirit.

Thinkers who do not do this by this very fact are not philosophers. Not believing the ideas constructed by the soul on the foundation of inspirations of the spirit, they act unjustly by not believing that this is what makes up the composition of the spirit, for the one thing is man’s work, while the other is God’s.
In the active it is from the action of the soul that there appears desirefor andproduction ofunselfsh deeds or virtues, or even higher than these—the yearning to become virtuous.

Properly, the work of the soul in this part (the will) is the establishment of temporal living conditions for man, so that he will be well-off. In fulfilling this function, the soul does everything according to the conviction that what is being done is either pleasant, or beneficial, or necessary for the living conditions which are being established. At the same time, it is not satisfied with this, but goes out of this domain and carries out deeds and undertakings, not at all because they are necessary, beneficial or pleasant, but because they are good, virtuous and just, striving towards them with all zeal, in spite of the fact that they contribute nothing toward temporal life and are even
